content.problemStatement:
You are a generous parent and have a collection of cookies to give to your children. Each child can receive **at most one cookie**.

Each child iii has a **greed factor** g[i]g[i]g[i], which is the minimum size of a cookie that will make the child happy. Each cookie jjj has a **size** s[j]s[j]s[j]. A child will be content if the size of the cookie they receive is **greater than or equal to** their greed factor.

Your task is to **maximize the number of content children** and return that number.

Function DeclarationFunction NamemaxContentChildrenmaxContentChildrenmaxContentChildren ‚Äì This function calculates the maximum number of children that can be made content by distributing cookies such that each child receives at most one cookie, and the cookie size is at least the child‚Äôs greed factor.

Parameters
ggg : A reference to an integer array representing the greed factor of each child.
sss : A reference to an integer array representing the sizes of available cookies.
Return Value
Returns an integer representing the maximum number of content children.
Constraints
1‚â§T‚â§101 \leq T \leq 101‚â§T‚â§10
1‚â§n‚â§3√ó1041 \leq n \leq 3 \times 10^41‚â§n‚â§3√ó104 (number of children)
0‚â§m‚â§3√ó1040 \leq m \leq 3 \times 10^40‚â§m‚â§3√ó104 (number of cookies)
1‚â§g[i],s[j]‚â§231‚àí11 \leq g[i], s[j] \leq 2^{31} - 11‚â§g[i],s[j]‚â§231‚àí1

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT ‚Äî the number of test cases.
For each test case:

The first line contains two integers nnn and mmm ‚Äî number of children and number of cookies.
The next line contains nnn integers ‚Äî greed factors of the children.
The next line contains mmm integers ‚Äî sizes of the cookies.




Output Format

For each test case, output a single integer ‚Äî the maximum number of content children.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: Children have greed factors [2,3,1,4] and cookies [1,3,2].

Assign cookie 1 -> child with greed 1
Assign cookie 2 -> child with greed 2
Assign cookie 3 -> child with greed 3
The child with greed 4 cannot be satisfied. Maximum content children = 3



Test case 2: Children [1,2,2] and cookies [2,3,1,4,5]

Assign cookie 1 -> child with greed 1
Assign cookie 2 -> child with greed 2
Assign cookie 3 -> child with greed 2
All children are content. Maximum content children = 3

solution.approach:
**Sort the children‚Äôs greed factors** in ascending order.




**Sort the cookies** in ascending order.




Use **two pointers**:



One pointer iterates through the children.
Another pointer iterates through the cookies.



For each child, try to find the **smallest cookie** that is large enough.



If the current cookie satisfies the child, increment both pointers (child is content, move to next cookie).
If not, move only the cookie pointer to try the next larger cookie.



Continue until all children or all cookies are considered.




The number of satisfied children is the final position of the child pointer.



Why This Works

**Greedy strategy**: Always give the smallest sufficient cookie to the current child.




Sorting ensures that:



Children with smaller greed are matched first, avoiding unnecessary wastage of larger cookies.
Cookies are considered in increasing order, ensuring the smallest one is used whenever possible.



This guarantees the **maximum number of children can be satisfied**.



Time and Space Complexity
**Sorting children and cookies**: `O(n log n + m log m)` where `n` is number of children and `m` is number of cookies.
**Matching with two pointers**: `O(n + m)`.
**Total complexity**: `O(n log n + m log m)`
**Space complexity**: `O(1)` if sorting is in-place.
Edge Cases to Consider
**No cookies** ‚Üí All children remain unsatisfied.
**No children** ‚Üí Nothing to satisfy.
**All cookies too small** ‚Üí No child can be satisfied.
**All children greed ‚â§ smallest cookie** ‚Üí All children can be satisfied.
**More cookies than children** ‚Üí Some cookies remain unused.
**All children greedier than largest cookie** ‚Üí No child can be satisfied.
SummaryThe problem can be efficiently solved with a **greedy approach** combined with sorting. The key idea is to **match children starting from the least greedy with the smallest available cookie** that can satisfy them. This guarantees the maximum number of satisfied children with minimal wasted resources.
